title: 🏠 Home Assistant Setup From Zero in 30 Minutes (2026)
description:
Step-by-step setup guide for Home Assistant on a Raspberry Pi 5. From flashing the SD card to first automation. Backed by official documentation and current community best practices.

Home Assistant setup, Raspberry Pi smart home, HACS install, Zigbee2MQTT, local smart home, open source automation.

segments.voice: Stop. Your smart home setup has a critical privacy flaw. Every device you bought from Amazon, Google, or Apple phones home with your behavior data. Home Assistant fixes this. In the next twenty-five minutes, I'm going to show you a complete setup on a Raspberry Pi 5 that costs under one hundred fifty dollars and runs entirely on your local network. No cloud required, no subscription, no spying.
segments.visual: Raspberry Pi 5 board components close up

segments.voice: Hardware list first. Raspberry Pi 5 with four gigabytes of RAM costs sixty dollars. Power supply with the new USB-C profile costs twelve dollars. Sixty-four gigabyte high-endurance SD card runs fifteen dollars. The official case with active cooling costs sixteen dollars. A Sonoff Zigbee 3.0 dongle to talk to wireless devices runs eighteen dollars. Total hardware just over one hundred twenty dollars.
segments.visual: Raspberry Pi accessories case SD card USB stick

segments.voice: Why a Raspberry Pi 5 specifically. The Pi 5 has roughly double the performance of the Pi 4 and handles Home Assistant smoothly even with twenty plus integrations. The Pi 4 still works for small setups but starts to lag with database queries on busy systems. Skip the Pi Zero and Pi 3, they don't have the memory for modern Home Assistant.
segments.visual: Raspberry Pi 5 vs 4 comparison benchmark

segments.voice: Step one, flash the SD card. Download Raspberry Pi Imager from the official website. Insert your SD card into your computer. Open Imager, click Choose Device and select Raspberry Pi 5. Click Choose OS, then Other specific purpose OS, then Home Assistants and Home Automation, then Home Assistant OS for Pi 5. Click Choose Storage and pick your SD card. Click Write. Takes about five minutes.
segments.visual: Raspberry Pi Imager software interface laptop

segments.voice: Step two, first boot. Insert the flashed SD card into the Pi 5. Connect ethernet, then plug in power. The first boot takes between ten and twenty minutes as Home Assistant downloads and installs its core packages. You'll see no display because Home Assistant OS is headless. Don't unplug it, just wait.
segments.visual: Raspberry Pi booting LED indicator powered up

segments.voice: Step three, find the Pi on your network. Open your router's admin page or use a network scanner like Fing on your phone. Look for a device named homeassistant or with a hostname starting with raspberrypi. Note the IP address. Type that IP followed by colon eight one two three into your browser. The Home Assistant onboarding screen appears.
segments.visual: router admin page IP address list devices network

segments.voice: Step four, create your account. Choose a strong password and store it in a password manager. This account has full admin rights to your home. Don't reuse passwords here. Home Assistant will prompt you to enter your location for sun rise and sun set automations. Set your time zone correctly because automation triggers depend on it.
segments.visual: Home Assistant onboarding wizard account creation

segments.voice: Step five, integrate your existing devices. Home Assistant will auto-discover devices on your network through mDNS, UPnP, and SSDP. You'll likely see your TV, your printer, your Sonos speakers, and possibly your router. Each gets a one-click setup. If you have Philips Hue, Ecobee, Nest, or Ring devices, those integrate through their respective integration cards.
segments.visual: Home Assistant device discovery integration cards

segments.voice: Step six, add the Zigbee dongle. Shut down Home Assistant from the Settings menu. Unplug power. Plug in the Sonoff Zigbee 3.0 USB dongle to one of the Pi's USB ports. Power back on. Once Home Assistant boots, go to Settings, then Devices and Services, then Add Integration, search for Zigbee Home Automation, and follow the prompts to detect the dongle.
segments.visual: USB Zigbee dongle plugged into Raspberry Pi

segments.voice: Step seven, pair your first Zigbee device. Buy an Aqara contact sensor for twelve dollars from any major retailer. In Home Assistant under Devices, click Add Device. Hold the small button on the contact sensor for five seconds until it blinks rapidly. Within twenty seconds, the sensor appears in Home Assistant. Name it Front Door Sensor. This is your first cloudless smart device.
segments.visual: Aqara contact sensor pairing button blue light

segments.voice: Step eight, install HACS. The Home Assistant Community Store unlocks thousands of community integrations and dashboard themes that aren't in the official integration list. Open the Terminal add-on, paste the install command from the HACS website, and after a restart, HACS appears in your sidebar. This is what makes Home Assistant a true platform.
segments.visual: HACS Home Assistant Community Store interface

segments.voice: Step nine, set up backups. From Settings, click System, then Backups. Create a full backup. Configure the Google Drive Backup add-on from the HACS store, or use the official Home Assistant Cloud subscription if you want managed backups. Schedule one nightly. SD cards fail, and a current backup means a thirty minute recovery instead of a weekend of pain.
segments.visual: backup configuration cloud storage schedule

segments.voice: Step ten, your first automation. Go to Settings, Automations, Create Automation, Start with empty automation. Set the trigger to State Change, choose your front door sensor, set From closed To open. Add an action of Notify, choose your phone via the companion app you'll install in a minute. Save and test by opening your door. The notification arrives in under three seconds.
segments.visual: automation builder interface trigger action setup

segments.voice: The companion app is essential. Install Home Assistant Companion from the App Store or Google Play. Sign in with your local account. Grant location, notification, and bluetooth permissions. This app turns your phone into a sensor that reports battery, location, motion, and step count to Home Assistant. Now you can build geofence automations.
segments.visual: Home Assistant companion app phone screen

segments.voice: Add a presence detection automation. Trigger when your phone leaves home zone. Action to turn off all lights and set thermostat to away mode. This single automation saves more energy than most paid smart home subscriptions promise. And it runs entirely on your Pi, no internet required for the trigger or action.
segments.visual: phone location GPS leaving home automation

segments.voice: Step eleven, dashboards. The default Lovelace dashboard auto-generates from your devices. To customize, click the three dots in the upper right of any dashboard, choose Edit Dashboard, then add cards. The Mushroom card collection from HACS is the most popular modern theme. The Button-card from HACS gives full design control if you want a magazine-quality interface.
segments.visual: dashboard customization cards Mushroom theme

segments.voice: Add the Mini Graph Card to see sensor data over time. Add the Weather card for forecasts. Add the Glance card to see all door and window sensors in one place. Spend an evening tweaking your dashboard. The visual customization is part of what makes Home Assistant feel like a system you own rather than rent.
segments.visual: Mini Graph Card dashboard data visualization

segments.voice: Step twelve, voice control. The Home Assistant Voice Preview Edition launched in early twenty twenty-five for fifty-nine dollars. It runs voice processing locally on the Pi using the Whisper and Piper engines. No cloud required, no recording sent to Amazon or Google. Setup takes ten minutes through the Voice integration card.
segments.visual: Home Assistant Voice device preview edition

segments.voice: Alternative voice options include the original Espressif Atom Echo for thirteen dollars, which is a tiny DIY voice satellite. Mic quality is mediocre but the price is right for proof of concept. For higher quality, the Onju Voice or the M5Stack PoE Cam options work with Home Assistant Voice through community projects on HACS.
segments.visual: Atom Echo small voice satellite device

segments.voice: Step thirteen, secure remote access. Don't expose Home Assistant to the public internet directly. Use Tailscale or WireGuard to create a private VPN back to your home network. Tailscale is the easiest, free for personal use, and takes ten minutes to set up. Install the Tailscale add-on, follow the prompts, and you can access Home Assistant from anywhere safely.
segments.visual: Tailscale VPN secure remote access laptop

segments.voice: Common mistakes to avoid. Don't use the default port forwarding setup that older guides recommend, it exposes your home to bots. Don't ignore SD card health, replace yours every two years or move to an SSD. Don't manually edit YAML files unless you have backups, one wrong indentation can take down the system. Use the visual editor when possible.
segments.visual: warning sign mistake do not enter security

segments.voice: When you want to upgrade beyond a Pi, the most popular paths are an Intel NUC for around three hundred dollars, a HomeAssistant Yellow that ships with Pi compatibility and an integrated PoE plus Zigbee chip for two hundred fifty dollars, or a HomeAssistant Green which is plug and play for one hundred fifty dollars but harder to extend.
segments.visual: Intel NUC HomeAssistant Yellow Green comparison

segments.voice: ESPHome is the next step after you're comfortable with Home Assistant basics. It lets you build cheap custom sensors using two dollar ESP32 chips. A DIY soil moisture sensor, a custom doorbell, a window vibration alarm. The ESPHome add-on installs from the integration store and the visual editor handles most projects without coding.
segments.visual: ESP32 chips sensors DIY electronics breadboard

segments.voice: Performance maintenance tips. Run the Database Health add-on monthly to keep the recorder database small. Disable history for sensors you don't care about graphing. Use the System Monitor card to watch your Pi's CPU and memory usage. If you hit ninety percent regularly, time to upgrade to an Intel NUC.
segments.visual: system monitor CPU memory performance graph

segments.voice: But there's one thing manufacturers hide. Home Assistant exposes integrations that the original manufacturer apps deliberately limit. The Sonos integration shows internal diagnostic data. The Tesla integration unlocks features hidden in the official app. The discovery rate of forgotten features is half the fun of moving to Home Assistant.
segments.visual: Home Assistant hidden features unlocked integrations

segments.voice: Subscribe to EspacioInteligente if you want the next video which goes deep on Zigbee versus Z-Wave versus Wi-Fi mesh, including range tests and reliability data from six months of logging. Drop a comment with the brand of device you tried to integrate first because that tells me which guides to make next.
segments.visual: subscribe button bell notifications YouTube channel
